significance.explanation: Opening morning heats of both 100m sprints carry Olympic stakes for athletes chasing advancement, though medals remain multiple rounds away. Paris champions Julien Alfred and Noah Lyles competing in their first strides adds prestige to preliminary rounds.

experience.explanation: Morning session at the Coliseum lacks prime-time energy and crowds, though the venue's iconic status and historic track provide undeniable context. A 9am start means cooler temperatures but less electric atmosphere than evening finals.

starPower.explanation: Defending Olympic champions Noah Lyles, Kishane Thompson, and Julien Alfred are globally recognized sprinters likely competing in their opening heats. Top-tier track talent guaranteed, though stars often rotate through prelims across multiple days.

uniqueness.explanation: While this is the first track action at the Coliseum in 2028, the session itself is one of multiple preliminary rounds for the 100m and field qualifications—a routine Olympic pattern rather than a once-in-a-lifetime pairing.

demand.explanation: Opening morning session at an iconic venue attracts strong interest, but early-round non-medal events and the 9am time slot mean this tracks as less competitive to book than evening finals, despite Olympic scarcity ensuring strong presale.

scorecard.overall: ATH01 opens track and field at the historic Coliseum with star-power in the sprint heats, but the morning preliminary format and multi-round structure place it solidly in the accessible-entry-point tier—an Olympic session with earned stakes for athletes rather than medals-at-stake for spectators.

significance.explanation: This session crowns the first Olympic track and field gold medal in Los Angeles since 1984 with the women's 100m final, the sport's flagship sprint event. The men's 10,000m final and women's shot put final add three total medal events to a marquee evening.

experience.explanation: Exceptional: women's 100m final under Saturday evening lights at the Coliseum—the fastest woman alive crowned in sub-11 seconds before 78,000 fans in the spiritual home of American track. Prime-time, iconic venue, and electric crowd energy combine at peak intensity.

starPower.explanation: Paris 100m gold medalist Julien Alfred and silver medalist Sha'Carri Richardson, both household names in global sprinting, headline a world-class women's 100m final. The men's 10,000m and shot put bring additional Olympic medalists.

uniqueness.explanation: Exceptional because: women's 100m final—the flagship sprint—at the Coliseum under evening lights for the first time since 1984 represents a once-in-a-lifetime pairing of sport, venue, and Olympic moment. The first LA medal awarded on American soil in 44 years.

demand.explanation: The women's 100m final at the Coliseum in prime time is among the most sought-after tickets of the entire Games, with price ceiling exceeding $2000 and near-certain sell-out. Olympic scarcity amplifies demand for the sport's signature sprint.

scorecard.overall: ATH02 is the marquee opening night of track and field at the Coliseum—women's 100m final featuring defending champions in prime time, plus two additional golds. This ranks among the most iconic sessions of LA28, commanding top-tier demand and delivering the spectacle that defines Olympic track.

significance.explanation: Decathlon Day 1 begins the two-day competition to crown the World's Greatest Athlete, with real Olympic stakes for advancing through the opening 100m, long jump, and shot put. Field qualifiers and the repechage carry advancement stakes but no medals yet.

experience.explanation: Sunday morning decathlon action at the Coliseum provides the unique appeal of watching an Olympic all-around test unfold across multiple simultaneous events, though the 9am time slot limits crowd energy and the daytime setting lacks prime-time drama.

starPower.explanation: Paris shot put and long jump medalists Ryan Crouser and Miltiadis Tentoglou are world-class field athletes competing in qualification rounds. Decathletes are national champions and elite all-around performers, though rarely household names compared to sprinters.

uniqueness.explanation: The decathlon format itself is distinctive, but this is one of two decathlon sessions across the Games with the same event structure. The mixed 4x400m relay is a newer format, though it will repeat across multiple qualifying rounds.

demand.explanation: Decathlon's unique appeal and the Coliseum location attract dedicated track enthusiasts, but the Sunday morning time and preliminary-round structure mean this is easier to book relative to evening finals, though Olympic scarcity ensures presale strength.

scorecard.overall: ATH03 introduces the decathlon's two-day drama and features world-class field athletes in qualifications, but the morning preliminary format and lack of medal decisions position it as a mid-tier session—fascinating for track aficionados, accessible entry point for casual fans seeking an Olympic experience.

significance.explanation: Exceptional: Noah Lyles chases gold in the men's 100m final on home soil—the flagship event of the entire Olympics. The session also crowns the men's shot put champion (Ryan Crouser potentially seeking an unprecedented third gold), plus decathlon Day 2 conclusion with the brutal 1500m final that determines the World's Greatest Athlete.

experience.explanation: Exceptional: American sprint royalty (Noah Lyles) competing under Sunday evening lights at the Coliseum—the same track where Carl Lewis became a legend. The 100m final lasts under 10 seconds of pure speed before 78,000 screaming fans. This is Olympic track and field at maximum intensity.

starPower.explanation: Paris 100m gold medalist Noah Lyles and silver medalist Kishane Thompson headline a world-class men's 100m final. Ryan Crouser, a two-time defending shot put champion, represents elite global field talent. The decathlon 1500m features world-class middle-distance runners.

uniqueness.explanation: Exceptional: men's 100m final at the Coliseum under evening lights represents an iconic pairing of marquee event, historic venue, and American home advantage. The decathlon conclusion and shot put final deepen the uniqueness—multiple medal rounds in one legendary evening.

demand.explanation: The men's 100m final at the Coliseum in prime time is among the absolute most sought-after tickets of the entire Games. Price ceiling exceeds $2000+. Noah Lyles on home soil ensures near-certain sell-out before presale closes.

scorecard.overall: ATH04 is the apex of track and field at LA28—men's 100m final with defending champion Noah Lyles at the Coliseum, plus the shot put final and decathlon conclusion. This ranks as one of the most iconic sessions of the entire Games, combining marquee sport, historic venue, and American superstar in prime time.

significance.explanation: The women's triple jump final awards a medal with defending champion Thea LaFond of Dominica in contention. Decathlon Day 2 features the brutal 110m hurdles, discus, and pole vault where leads can shift dramatically. Repechage rounds offer second chances but carry preliminary stakes.

experience.explanation: Monday morning at the Coliseum transitions to mid-meet rhythm with a mixed program of field and track events, though the 9am start limits crowd energy. demand.explanation: Decathlon enthusiasts and field event aficionados drive interest, but the Monday morning time slot and the triple jump (a less universally followed event) mean this is more accessible to book than evening prime-time finals, though Olympic scarcity supports presale activity.

scorecard.overall: ATH05 delivers decathlon Day 2 drama and the women's triple jump final, with strong technical field-event action at the Coliseum. significance.explanation: Three gold medal events: men's pole vault final (with world record holder Mondo Duplantis), women's 800m final (defending Olympic champion Keely Hodgkinson), and the decathlon's 1500m final that crowns the World's Greatest Athlete. The mixed 4x100m relay final adds a medal-deciding round.

experience.explanation: Exceptional: Mondo Duplantis in the men's pole vault final under Monday evening lights at the Coliseum—a marquee field event featuring a world record holder with genuine potential to make history. The decathlon 1500m final and women's 800m add layered drama to a loaded evening.

starPower.explanation: Mondo Duplantis is a globally recognized pole vault icon and world record holder competing for his third consecutive Olympic gold. uniqueness.explanation: Mondo Duplantis's pole vault final at the Coliseum—a marquee field event at an iconic venue with a genuine world record threat—represents a rare combination of athlete dominance and venue prestige. demand.explanation: Mondo Duplantis attracts global pole vault enthusiasts and record-chasers; the women's 800m final and decathlon conclusion drive strong secondary demand. Evening session at the Coliseum with price ceiling exceeding $1500 positions this as highly competitive to book.

scorecard.overall: ATH06 is a strong marquee evening with Mondo Duplantis's pole vault final under lights—a world-class athlete capable of historic moments—paired with the women's 800m and decathlon finale. While not quite the sprint-marquee tier of ATH04, this delivers legitimate gold-medal stakes and star power across multiple events.

significance.explanation: Qualification rounds across five events (pole vault, steeplechase, 400m, triple jump, 110m hurdles) carry advancement stakes for Olympic athletes, though no medals are decided. Paris 400m champion Marileidy Paulino and defending pole vault champion Nina Kennedy may be advancing through standards rather than competing for gold.

experience.explanation: Tuesday morning at the Coliseum during mid-week hours lacks prime-time energy and crowds. The Coliseum's iconic status provides context, but early-morning prelims with daytime lighting and dispersed spectators represent the lowest-energy Olympic track experience at this venue.

starPower.explanation: Paris pole vault champion Nina Kennedy and defending 400m champion Marileidy Paulino are globally recognized if competing. Men's triple jump qualifiers include Paris medalists Jordan Díaz and Pedro Pichardo. Strong field talent throughout, though stars often rotate through multiple preliminary sessions.

uniqueness.explanation: These are routine qualification rounds across five different events—the standard Olympic preliminary structure. While the Coliseum's history is iconic, one of many qualification mornings across the Games does not represent a distinctive or once-in-a-lifetime session.

demand.explanation: Qualification rounds and mid-morning timing make this the most accessible session yet to book. While Olympic scarcity supports presale, early-round non-medal sessions at daytime hours represent the lowest relative demand tier, with price ceiling around $1000.

scorecard.overall: ATH07 is an Olympic-baseline morning session—athletes advancing through qualifications, iconic venue providing context, but lacking the medal stakes, prime-time energy, or star concentration that drives marquee demand. This is track and field for specialists willing to watch preliminary rounds.

significance.explanation: Four gold medal events: men's high jump final, men's discus final, women's 5000m final, and women's 800m final (defending champion Keely Hodgkinson). Semifinals in the 400m hurdles and 1500m set medal-round fields. The men's 200m begins early rounds.

experience.explanation: Strong: four championship finals packed into a Tuesday evening session at the Coliseum with prime-time lighting and strong crowd atmosphere. The mix of field events (high jump, discus) and distance racing (women's 5000m, 800m) provides varied spectacle, though the brief three-hour window compresses multiple events.

starPower.explanation: Defending women's 800m champion Keely Hodgkinson is globally recognized. Paris 200m gold medalist Letsile Tebogo may appear in early rounds. World-class field athletes and distance runners throughout. Star power is strong though concentrated more in established athletes than household-name sprinters.

uniqueness.explanation: While the Coliseum evening setting is iconic, four non-relay, non-sprint finals represent standard medal-round structure repeated across track and field. The session follows typical Olympic scheduling patterns rather than offering a distinctive or once-in-a-lifetime combination.

demand.explanation: Four gold medal events under evening lights at the Coliseum drive strong demand, with price ceiling exceeding $1500. Keely Hodgkinson's 800m final and the 5000m add international appeal. This is a competitive-to-book evening without reaching the absolute-scarcest sprint-final tier.

scorecard.overall: ATH08 is a strong marquee evening delivering four golds with defending champion Keely Hodgkinson anchoring the women's 800m, plus compelling distance and field finals. The concentration of medal events and prime-time venue elevate it to high-demand, though the mix of non-flagship sports places it slightly below sprint-marquee nights.

significance.explanation: The women's 3000m steeplechase final awards a gold medal in a grueling event requiring 35 barriers and seven water jumps. Repechage rounds in the 400m and 200m offer second chances for athletes seeking medal races. Field qualifications carry advancement stakes.

experience.explanation: Wednesday morning at the Coliseum places this session at mid-week and daytime hours, limiting crowd energy. The steeplechase final is distinctive—runners clearing barriers and splashing through water—but the 9:35am start means cooler crowds and less electric atmosphere than prime-time finals.

starPower.explanation: The steeplechase features elite endurance runners and national champions, though rarely household names globally. Paris triple jump medalists Tara Davis-Woodhall and Malaika Mihambo are recognized athletes if advancing through long jump qualification. Star power is solid within track-specialist circles.

uniqueness.explanation: The steeplechase final is a single medal event at the Coliseum—while the discipline is distinctive, this follows standard final structure. Repechage rounds are new for LA28 but represent routine preliminary opportunities rather than once-in-a-lifetime moments.

demand.explanation: The steeplechase final attracts dedicated distance-running enthusiasts, but the Wednesday morning slot and focus on a niche event mean this is more accessible to book than evening prime-time sessions, though Olympic scarcity supports presale activity at moderate price ceiling (~$1000).

scorecard.overall: ATH09 features the distinctive spectacle of the steeplechase final—a grueling, unpredictable distance event—but the mid-week morning timing and single marquee medal event position this as a solid mid-tier session for distance-running aficionados rather than a mainstream draw.

significance.explanation: Exceptional: the 1500m final—Olympic royalty, the metric mile that produces the most dramatic Olympic track finishes. Three additional golds: men's long jump (defending champion Miltiadis Tentoglou), women's discus, and men's 400m hurdles final. The women's 200m begins rounds with Paris gold medalist Gabby Thomas potentially competing.

experience.explanation: Exceptional: four championship finals packed into Wednesday evening at the Coliseum, with the 1500m final as the centerpiece—a tactical masterpiece that explodes in chaos over the final lap. Field events (long jump, discus) plus sprint hurdles provide varied spectacle under prime-time lights before 78,000 fans.

starPower.explanation: The 1500m final features Jakob Ingebrigtsen (Paris 5000m champion) and Josh Kerr (Paris 1500m silver), both globally recognized middle-distance stars. Miltiadis Tentoglou is the defending long jump champion. Gabby Thomas (Paris 200m gold) may race. World-class athletes across multiple disciplines.

uniqueness.explanation: The 1500m final at the Coliseum—Olympic track and field's most dramatic distance event—at an iconic venue commands distinctive appeal. While medal-round structure is standard, the 1500m's legendary status and tactical intensity elevate this session's uniqueness significantly.

demand.explanation: The 1500m final under evening lights at the Coliseum is among the most sought-after track tickets, with price ceiling exceeding $1500. Four golds and Ingebrigtsen/Kerr rivalry drive intense competition for booking. This ranks as highly competitive to secure.

scorecard.overall: ATH10 is a marquee evening anchored by the legendary 1500m final—Olympic track's most dramatic middle-distance race—plus three additional golds including defending long jump champion Tentoglou. The combination of iconic event, star athletes, and prime-time venue position this as one of the most compelling track sessions.

significance.explanation: The men's 3000m steeplechase final awards a gold medal in a grueling endurance event. Women's high jump qualification and men's 800m heats carry advancement stakes, with Paris 800m champion Emmanuel Wanyonyi potentially competing if he races. Field qualifications represent preliminary rounds.

experience.explanation: Thursday morning at the Coliseum features the steeplechase final as a dramatic centerpiece—runners clearing barriers and splashing through water—but the 9:30am start limits crowd energy and daytime lighting. A solid Olympic session without the electric prime-time atmosphere.

starPower.explanation: The steeplechase features elite endurance runners and Olympic medalists, though rarely household names. Paris 800m champion Emmanuel Wanyonyi is recognized within running circles if competing. World-class field talent throughout, though star concentration is modest compared to sprint events.

uniqueness.explanation: The steeplechase final is a single medal event at the Coliseum—distinctive in format but following standard final structure. Qualification rounds and 800m heats represent routine preliminary sessions. This is one of several morning sessions across the Games without distinctive positioning.

demand.explanation: The steeplechase final attracts distance-running enthusiasts, but Thursday morning timing and the focus on a niche event mean this is more accessible to book than evening finals, though Olympic scarcity ensures presale strength at moderate price ceiling (~$1000).

scorecard.overall: ATH11 centers on the distinctive men's steeplechase final—a grueling, captivating distance event—but the morning preliminary structure and limited star concentration position this as a solid mid-tier session for track specialists rather than a mainstream primetime draw.

significance.explanation: Exceptional: five gold medal events in one evening—women's pole vault final (defending champion Nina Kennedy), men's hammer final, women's 400m hurdles final (Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone could be defending with a world record), and semifinals in the 110m hurdles, 200m, and both 400m races that set fields for later golds.

experience.explanation: Strong: five championship finals compressed into Thursday evening at the Coliseum, with prime-time lights and strong crowd energy. The pole vault (bar rising until one vaulter remains), hammer throw (biggest arm circles in sport), and 400m hurdles provide distinctive spectacle across multiple event types.

starPower.explanation: Defending women's pole vault champion Nina Kennedy is globally recognized. Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone (world record holder in 400m hurdles) and Femke Bol (Dutch 400m hurdles star) represent elite sprint-hurdlers. Paris 200m champion Letsile Tebogo and other world-class athletes throughout. Strong star concentration.

uniqueness.explanation: Five championship finals in one evening at the Coliseum is a compressed, high-stakes format. The women's 400m hurdles final featuring the world record holder and the pole vault under lights provide distinctive appeal. The concentration of multiple golds elevates the session's uniqueness.

demand.explanation: Five gold medal events under evening lights at the Coliseum drive exceptional demand. Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone and Nina Kennedy attract global audiences. Price ceiling exceeds $1500. This is among the most competitive-to-book evening track sessions.

scorecard.overall: ATH12 is a marquee evening delivering five golds with defending champions (Nina Kennedy, Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone) and world record holders competing. The compression of multiple medal events and the distinctive appeal of pole vault and 400m hurdles position this as one of the most sought-after track evenings.

significance.explanation: The men's triple jump final awards a gold medal with Paris champion Jordan Díaz of Spain in contention. Heptathlon Day 1 begins the two-day grind to crown the world's best female all-around athlete with high jump and 100m hurdles. Women's 100m hurdles heats and men's 5000m rounds carry advancement stakes.

experience.explanation: Friday morning at the Coliseum features heptathlon's opening events and the men's triple jump final, providing distinctive multi-event spectacle. The 9:35am start limits crowd energy and daytime lighting reduces atmosphere. Solid Olympic experience without prime-time intensity.

starPower.explanation: Paris triple jump champion Jordan Díaz is a world-class field athlete. Heptathletes are elite all-around performers and national champions. World-class distance runners in the 5000m rounds. Star power is solid within track circles, though without household-name sprint celebrities.

uniqueness.explanation: The heptathlon format is distinctive, but this is one of two heptathlon sessions across the Games. The men's triple jump final is a standard medal round at the Coliseum. The session follows routine Friday morning preliminary patterns rather than offering unique Olympic positioning.

demand.explanation: Heptathlon enthusiasts and field event aficionados drive interest, but the Friday morning slot and mixed program of early rounds mean this is more accessible to book than evening finals, though Olympic scarcity supports presale at moderate price ceiling (~$1000).

scorecard.overall: ATH13 introduces heptathlon Day 1 with distinctive all-around athlete competition and features Paris triple jump champion Díaz, but the Friday morning format and preliminary structure position this as a solid session for track specialists rather than a mainstream primetime attraction.

significance.explanation: Exceptional: four gold medal events—men's 110m hurdles final, men's 200m final (Paris champion Letsile Tebogo potentially defending against Noah Lyles), women's 10000m final, and heptathlon Day 2 shot put/200m completion. Women's 1500m and 200m semifinals set additional medal-race fields. This is peak track medal density.

experience.explanation: Exceptional: four championship finals under Friday evening lights at the Coliseum, with the men's 200m rematch (Tebogo vs. Lyles) and women's 10000m as centerpieces. Heptathlon concludes with explosive events. Prime-time energy, iconic venue, and varied event types create electric atmosphere.

starPower.explanation: Men's 200m final features Paris gold medalist Letsile Tebogo (age 22, shocking the world) and Paris bronze medalist Noah Lyles (defending 100m champion). Gabby Thomas (Paris 200m gold) may race semifinals. Sifan Hassan (multi-event legend attempting another masterpiece) in the 10000m final. Exceptional star concentration.

uniqueness.explanation: The men's 200m rematch of Tebogo (Paris gold) vs. Lyles (home athlete, defending 100m champion) at the Coliseum under evening lights is a distinctive athletic narrative. The women's 10000m and heptathlon completion add layered appeal. Four golds in one session elevate uniqueness.

demand.explanation: The Tebogo vs. Lyles 200m rematch on home soil drives exceptional demand. Four golds, evening lights, Coliseum venue, and global star concentration create top-tier scarcity. Price ceiling exceeds $2000. This ranks among the most competitive-to-book sessions of the entire Games.

scorecard.overall: ATH14 is an exceptional evening anchored by the men's 200m rematch (Tebogo's shock Paris gold vs. Lyles' home advantage) plus three additional golds including women's 10000m and heptathlon finale. The combination of marquee event, star athletes, narrative tension, and prime-time venue position this as among the most sought-after track sessions.

significance.explanation: Exceptional: five gold medal events in one afternoon/evening—women's 200m final (Paris champion Gabby Thomas potentially defending on home soil), women's 400m final (Paris champion Marileidy Paulino), men's 400m final (Paris champion Quincy Hall), women's long jump final (Paris champion Tara Davis-Woodhall at home), and heptathlon completion awarding the World's Greatest Female Athlete.

experience.explanation: Exceptional: five championship finals packed into Saturday afternoon/evening at the Coliseum, transitioning from daytime to prime-time lights. The mix of sprints (200m, 400m), field (long jump), and heptathlon finale provides unrelenting spectacle before 78,000 fans under Olympic lights.

starPower.explanation: Paris 200m gold medalist Gabby Thomas (American, home advantage, multi-gold medalist), Paris 400m champions Quincy Hall and Marileidy Paulino, Paris long jump champion Tara Davis-Woodhall (competing at home), and heptathlon's world-class all-around athlete. Exceptional global star concentration across all disciplines.

uniqueness.explanation: Five championship finals at the Coliseum in one session is extraordinarily dense medal-round programming. Gabby Thomas (Paris triple-gold medalist) racing 200m at home, Tara Davis-Woodhall defending long jump at home, and the heptathlon conclusion represent distinctive narrative threads unique to this session.

demand.explanation: Five gold medal events under afternoon/evening lights with three American athletes defending/competing at home (Thomas, Hall, Davis-Woodhall) create exceptional demand. The heptathlon finale and compressed medal density drive competition for remaining tickets. Price ceiling exceeds $2000. This is among the absolute most sought-after sessions.

scorecard.overall: ATH15 is the crescendo of track and field at LA28—five golds including American athletes defending medals on home soil (Gabby Thomas, Tara Davis-Woodhall, Quincy Hall), plus the heptathlon's crowning of the world's best female all-around athlete. This represents the peak density of medal events and star athletes, making it one of the most iconic sessions of the entire Games.

significance.explanation: Eight medal events across sprints, middle distance, jumps, and throws constitute one of the deepest single sessions of the entire Games. Faith Kipyegon's potential third consecutive 1500m gold and Emmanuel Wanyonyi's 800m title defense carry historic weight; the men's and women's relays add national prestige to the medal stakes.

experience.explanation: Exceptional combination of the world's most storied track venue, prime Sunday evening timing (3:40–6:15 PM), rapid-fire medal events that span sprints through distance running, and the Coliseum's 78,000-seat energy. This is the penultimate night — crowds will be at peak intensity and the relay finale will be electric.

starPower.explanation: Faith Kipyegon defending her two-time 1500m title, Emmanuel Wanyonyi defending 800m gold, Yaroslava Mahuchikh returning to high jump, and the USA men's relay squad chasing gold on home soil all but guarantee globally-recognized athletes competing across every event block.

uniqueness.explanation: The Coliseum's historic third Olympic hosting and the pairing of a potential three-peat Olympic title with one of the deepest single-session medal lineups elevate this beyond routine relay nights. The convergence of middle-distance history, vertical drama, and relay legacy at this specific venue creates a genuinely memorable session.

demand.explanation: Exceptional because it combines the Coliseum's iconic status, prime evening slot, eight medal events, and the narrative weight of Kipyegon's potential three-peat and USA relay gold pursuit on home soil. Price ceiling extends to $2460, and scarcity will drive competitive booking across all tiers.

scorecard.overall: This is one of the signature nights of the entire LA28 Games — a deep, star-laden session at the world's most storied track venue during prime evening hours on the penultimate night of the Games. The combination of historic personal narratives, national relay pride, and the Coliseum's three-Olympic legacy creates an unmissable experience.

significance.explanation: Six medal events across the entire spectrum of Olympic track and field — sprints, middle distance, throws, and hurdles — all on the final night of athletics at the Coliseum. Four relay finals in one session means national pride on the line across four separate medal races, plus the men's 5000m final as the marquee distance event.

experience.explanation: Exceptional because it is the final night of track and field at the Coliseum — a historic closing ceremony for one of sport's greatest venues hosting its third Olympic Games. Prime Monday evening (4:25–6:15 PM), six medal events, and the ceremonial weight of the last medals awarded under the peristyle arches create an unmatched live atmosphere.

starPower.explanation: Jakob Ingebrigtsen (if he qualifies for 5000m), strong international javelin and hurdles contenders, and the USA women's relay team — the Paris 4x100m gold medalists — returning on home soil all but guarantee world-class competitors. Multiple relay finals also ensure full squad rosters of Olympic-level sprinters competing.

uniqueness.explanation: This session is the ceremonial closer of track and field at a venue hosting its third Olympic Games in history — a genuinely once-in-a-lifetime convergence. The final medals awarded at the Coliseum in 2028 carry historical weight beyond the individual event stakes, and four relay finals in a single evening creates a rare concentration of baton drama.

demand.explanation: Exceptional demand driven by the closing-night ceremonial status, six medal events, four relay finals (including the USA women's 4x100m chasing consecutive gold), and the Coliseum's third-Olympic hosting milestone. This is the marquee closer to ten days of track history at the world's most iconic venue.

scorecard.overall: This is the crown jewel session of LA28 athletics — the final night of track and field at the Coliseum, combining six medal events, four relay finals, and the historical weight of closing 100 years of Olympic track at a three-time host venue. Demand and significance reach their peak; this is the session every American track fan will pursue.
